content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 25, 2006


Susan L. Adams, Ph.D., R.N.
Supervisor, First District
County of Marin
3501 Civic Center Drive, Ste. 329
San Rafael, CA 94903


RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-06-129

Dear Dr. Adams:

     This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance¹ regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").² Please note, the
Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A),
copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any
conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter
apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTION

     If a redevelopment plan for a six-acre area currently consisting primarily of a
derelict strip mall located 820 feet from your home comes before the Marin County
Board of Supervisors for consideration, may you participate in those decisions?

CONCLUSION

     From the facts you have provided it appears that your property would be
indirectly involved in governmental decisions relating to the strip mall area.
Accordingly, any financial effect of such decisions on your residence is presumed not to
be material and you may participate in these decisions unless the presumption is rebutted
by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its

___________________________
¹ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

² Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. 1-06-129
Page No. 2

financial effect, and the nature of your real property, which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on your real property.

FACTS

You are a member of the Marin County Board of Supervisors. You live in an
unincorporated part of your district in a neighborhood that is low density and suburban in
nature. Your district includes about 50,000 residents within less than a 5 mile radius.

Your home is approximately 820 feet from an area consisting primarily of a
derelict six-acre strip mall. An urban planning facilitator has been hired to bring the
community, the owners, the prospective developer and county planning staff together to
develop a community based redevelopment plan (the "Redevelopment Plan") for this area
(the "Redevelopment Area"). Approximately 1200 residents, including you, could be
affected by the Redevelopment Plan.

A proposed plan is shaping up to include a transit-oriented retail village with a
grocery store, bike/pedestrian access and up to 100 housing units, some for sale and
others for rent, with half of the homes being "affordable" (30 of them for low and very
low income people). The community as a whole will be meeting this month to review the
facilitated group's work and a first-draft conceptual proposal to be presented by the core
group. Ultimately, a proposal will come before the county board of supervisors for
consideration and a vote.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a governmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.

Step One. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of section 87100?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As a member of the Marin County
Board of Supervisors, a local government agency, you are a public official.

File No. 1-06-129
Page No. 3


Step Two. As a member of the board of supervisors will you be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision regarding the Redevelopment Area?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

Your facts presuppose you will be making or participating in making decisions regarding the Redevelopment Plan when these decisions come before the board of supervisors for a vote. In addition, when you appear at community discussion groups, if you engage in negotiations, provide advice or make recommendations without significant substantive review, you will be "participating in making" a governmental decision (regulation 18702.2) and you will also be "influencing" that decision if you appear before or otherwise attempt to influence any other members of the board of supervisors, or officers, employees or consultants of the county. (Regulation 18702.3.)

There is one possible exception that may apply that appears in regulation 18702.4(b)(1)(A)-(C). Even if a conflict of interest is present, a public official may appear before his or her agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function, as a member of the general public in order to represent himself or herself, but only in regard to an interest in real property which is wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family. Under this exception, you would be permitted to appear as a member of the general public at community meetings, informational meetings and workshops or hearings regarding the Redevelopment Plan, so long as you were solely representing your own personal interests. This is a very narrow exception and it does not permit you to address the interests of other property or business owners.³

³ Even if silent attendance at a board of supervisors meeting is prohibited by section 87105 and regulation 18702.5(b). Section 87105 and regulation 18702.5 require that if a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting, recuse himself or herself from voting and leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item.

File No. 1-06-129
Page No. 4

Step Three. What are your economic interests?

The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are
described in section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. The Act recognizes six kinds
of economic interests. However, since you are only asking about your real property
interest, we limit our analysis accordingly. Under section 87103(b) of the Act and
regulation 18703.2, a public official has an economic interest in real property in which
or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. Presumably, you have at least a
$2,000 economic interest in your home.4

Step Four. Will your economic interest be directly or indirectly involved in
decisions you will make, participate in making or attempt to influence as a member
of the board of supervisors?

Regulation 18704.2(a) sets forth six sets of circumstances under which real
property in which a public official has an economic interest is deemed to be directly
involved in a governmental decision:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest,
or any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the governmental decision.
For purposes of subdivision (a)(5), real property is located
'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries)
of the real property which is the subject of the governmental
decision' if any part of the real property is within 500 feet

There is a narrow exception to the rule requiring the public official to leave the room and which
allows him or her to speak at the meeting. Under regulation 18702.5(d)(3) a public official may speak as
any other member of the public to advocate on behalf of his or her own personal interests. To avail oneself
of this exception, the official must nevertheless, following the announcement of the agenda item to be
discussed or voted upon but before either the discussion or vote commences, publicly identify the type
of economic interest (or the like) (an interest in real property), and give the address or another indication
of the location of the property, the official's personal residence, in which case,
identification that the property is a residence will suffice. If the governmental decision is to be made
during an open session of a public meeting, his or her identification of his economic interest must be made
orally and shall be made part of the official public record. He or she must then leave the dais to speak from
the same area as members of the public. Under this exception, the public official's comments must be
limited to his or her personal interests, as defined in regulation 18702.4(b), and care should be taken to
clarify that this is not an appearance in any official capacity. The public official must be prohibited
from voting on the decisions and from privately discussing these matters with other members of the board
of supervisors or other county officials, employees or consultants.

4 A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. (Section
87103; regulation 18703.5.) However, under regulation 18705.5(a), when determining whether a
governmental decision has a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her
personal finances, a financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official
is not considered. Thus, we limit our discussion to possible financial effects on your real property interests.

File No. I-06-129
Page No. 5

of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment project area.

(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or
lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county,
district or other local governmental subdivision, of the
real property in which the official has an interest or a similar
decision affecting the real property. For purposes of this
subdivision, the terms "zoning" and "rezoning" shall refer to
the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land use
designation on the real property in which the official has an
interest.

(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial
or revocation of a license, permit or other land use
entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of the real
property in which the official has an interest.

(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition,
repeal or modification of any taxes or fees assessed or
imposed on the real property in which the official has an
interest.

(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan,
to form a project area committee, to certify the
environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan,
to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or
amend any of the above decisions; and real property in
which the official has an interest, or any part of it is located
within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment area.

(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements
to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities,
and the real property in which the official has an interest
will receive new or improved services."

You indicate that any redevelopment plan would be limited to the six-acre
development Area. From this we conclude that none of the six circumstances
described in regulation 18704.2(a) would come up and your real property would not be
directly involved in those governmental decisions. If a public official's interest in real

File No. I-06-129
Page No. 6

property is not directly involved in the governmental decision, it is deemed to be
indirectly involved.5

Steps Five and Six. Will the financial effects of a decision on the public
official's economic interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?

The materiality standard applicable to real property that is indirectly involved in a
governmental decision is governed by regulation 18705.2(b)(1) which provides that
"[t]he financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly
involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption
may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the
governmental decision, its financial effect, the nature of the real property in which
the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the official has
an interest." The regulation lists the following examples: the development potential or
income producing potential of the official's real property, the use of the official's real
property and the character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial
effects on traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar
traits of the neighborhood.

You have advised that your suburban low-density neighborhood of 1200 residents
may be affected by the Redevelopment Plan. You have also stated that the derelict strip
mall area will change to mixed use to include up to 100 housing units within a six-acre
area. Consequently, it appears that the decisions you will be making regarding the
Redevelopment Plan may result in a material financial effect on your residence by
changing the character of the neighborhood, thereby rebutting the presumption.6 Because
the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 71), it is ultimately up to you to decide whether your facts are sufficient to
rebut this presumption. If it is not rebutted, you may participate in decisions relating to
the Redevelopment Plan.

5 We cannot forecast what particular decisions may come before the board of supervisors in the
future. Accordingly, if a decision related to the Redevelopment Area has an effect falling within any of the
six sets of circumstances described above, your interest may be directly involved in that particular decision.

6 An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." Regulation 18706: In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of
a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made depends on the specific
facts surrounding the decision. (In re Thorner, supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.

File No. I-06-129
Page No. 7


Steps Seven and Eight. The "public generally" and "legally required
participation" exceptions.

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a governmental decision
may nevertheless participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception
applies when the financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is
substantially the same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. (Section
87103; regulation 18707.) The "legally required participation" rule applies when the
official's participation in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101;
regulation 18708.)

You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
your situation. Therefore, we do not address them.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: If a redevelopment plan for a six-acre area currently consisting primarily of a
derelict strip mall located 820 feet from your home comes before the Marin County
Board of Supervisors for consideration, may you participate in those decisions?

CONCLUSION: From the facts you have provided it appears that your property would be
indirectly involved in governmental decisions relating to the strip mall area.
Accordingly, any financial effect of such decisions on your residence is presumed not to
be material and you may participate in these decisions unless the presumption is rebutted

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 25, 2006 Susan L. Adams, Ph.D., R.N. Supervisor, First District County of Marin 3501 Civic Center Drive, Ste. 329 San Rafael, CA 94903 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-129 Dear Dr. Adams: This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance¹ regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").² Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. QUESTION If a redevelopment plan for a six-acre area currently consisting primarily of a derelict strip mall located 820 feet from your home comes before the Marin County Board of Supervisors for consideration, may you participate in those decisions? CONCLUSION From the facts you have provided it appears that your property would be indirectly involved in governmental decisions relating to the strip mall area. Accordingly, any financial effect of such decisions on your residence is presumed not to be material and you may participate in these decisions unless the presumption is rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its ___________________________ ¹ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed). ² Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. 1-06-129 Page No. 2 financial effect, and the nature of your real property, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on your real property. FACTS You are a member of the Marin County Board of Supervisors. You live in an unincorporated part of your district in a neighborhood that is low density and suburban in nature. Your district includes about 50,000 residents within less than a 5 mile radius. Your home is approximately 820 feet from an area consisting primarily of a derelict six-acre strip mall. An urban planning facilitator has been hired to bring the community, the owners, the prospective developer and county planning staff together to develop a community based redevelopment plan (the "Redevelopment Plan") for this area (the "Redevelopment Area"). Approximately 1200 residents, including you, could be affected by the Redevelopment Plan. A proposed plan is shaping up to include a transit-oriented retail village with a grocery store, bike/pedestrian access and up to 100 housing units, some for sale and others for rent, with half of the homes being "affordable" (30 of them for low and very low income people). The community as a whole will be meeting this month to review the facilitated group's work and a first-draft conceptual proposal to be presented by the core group. Ultimately, a proposal will
